comments.text: I have a forthcoming arxiv preprint that shows, using Vjeko_Kovac's notation, $\varepsilon(A) \le \exp\left(-c\sqrt{K\log K}\right)$. A simpler version of the same argument can also be used to show that $\varepsilon(A) \le \exp(-c\sqrt{K})$, and it's probably short enough that I can summarize it here: Choose $S$ maximizing $R(S)\le 1$ (again using Vjeko_Kovac's notation) and write $R(S)=1-\varepsilon$, $N=\varepsilon^{-1}$, $x=\log N$, and put $B=A\setminus S$. Then $R(B)>K-1$. Also every denominator in $B$ is $<N$, since otherwise some remaining term would have size $\le\varepsilon$ and could be added to $S$, contradicting maximality. We next compress $B$: whenever $p\mid n$ and there are $p$ copies of $n$, replace those $p$ copies by one formal copy of $n/p$. This preserves reciprocal mass, and every subset sum in the compressed multiset lifts to an actual subset sum in $B$. Let $C$ be a terminal compressed multiset, with multiplicities $m_n$. Then $R(C)>K-1$, all denominators in $C$ are $<N$, and $m_n<P^-(n)$ for every $n$, where $P^-(n)$ is the least prime factor of $n$. Also $n=1$ cannot occur, since that would already give an exact sum $1$. We use the following activation lemma: Let $E$ be a collection of distinct denominators in $C$ with $\alpha/2<m_n/n\le\alpha$, let $T=|E|$, and define $D(E)=\max_{q\le e^{C_0x}}\#\{n\in E:n\mid q\}$. If $H(E):=\sum_{n\in E}m_n/n\gg D(E)\sqrt{\alpha x}+\alpha D(E)x$, then the bounded sums $\sum_{n\in E}u_n/n$, $0\le u_n\le m_n$, contain a value in $(1-e^{-2x},1)$. Since $e^{-2x}<\varepsilon$, this contradicts the choice of $S$. The proof is as follows; choose a parameter $s$, activate each denominator $n\in E$ independently with probability $s/T$, and, if $n$ is activated, choose a coefficient uniformly from an interval ${0,\ldots,r_n}$ with $r_n\asymp n/s$, chosen so that the random sum $X=\sum u_n/n$ has mean approximately $1$. This is possible as long as $s\gg 1/\alpha$, which ensures $r_n\le m_n$. The variance of $X$ is $\asymp 1/s$. Let $t$ denote the Fourier frequency, so the characteristic function is $\phi_X(t)=\mathbb E \left[e^{itX}\right]$. For small frequencies $|t|\ll s$, Taylor expansion gives Gaussian decay $|\phi_X(t)|\le \exp(-ct^2/s)$. For larger frequencies, write $z=t/(2\pi)$ and note that a denominator $n$ is nearly resonant only when $z$ is close to a multiple of $n$. More precisely, if $\operatorname{dist}(z,n\mathbb Z)\le R$, then $n$ divides one of $O(R+1)$ integers near $z$, so by definition of $D(E)$ we have $\#\{n\in E:\operatorname{dist}(z,n\mathbb Z)\le R\}\ll (R+1)D(E)$. Sorting the distances from $z$ to the lattices $n\mathbb Z$ gives high-frequency decay $\exp(-c\cdot\min\{s,T^2/(D(E)^2s)\})$. Optimizing $s$ gives exactly the threshold $H(E)\gg D(E)\sqrt{\alpha x}+\alpha D(E)x$. Fourier inversion with a smooth nonnegative test function supported in $(1-e^{-2x},1)$ then shows that some bounded coefficient sum lies in that interval. It remains to sum the forced bounds over all denominator types in $C$. For prime denominators, $D(E)\ll x/\log x$, since an integer $q\le e^{C_0x}$ has $O(x/\log x)$ prime factors. Summing the activation bound over dyadic $\alpha$ gives total prime mass $O(x^2)$. For composite denominators, group dyadically by a denominator scale $P\le n<2P$ and by a multiplicity scale $m_n\asymp u$. Compression gives $P^-(n)>u/2$, hence $u\ll\sqrt P$ for composites. Moreover each such $n$ has at most $r(u)=\lfloor \log(2P)/\log(u/2)\rfloor$ prime factors, and therefore $D(E)\ll (C(1+x/\log P))^{r(u)}$. Applying the activation lemma to these cells and summing the dyadic $u$-levels gives $R(C\cap [P,2P))\ll (1+x/\log P)^2+\log P$. Summing this over dyadic $P<N$ gives total composite mass $O(x^2)$. Therefore $K-1<R(C)\ll x^2$, so $x\gg\sqrt K$. Since $x=\log(1/\varepsilon)$, this proves $\varepsilon\le \exp(-c\sqrt K)$. AI Acknowledgement: the random sum idea was mine (inspired by https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.07113 !), but the Fourier details were performed by GPT-5.5 and might warrant close inspection.
The paper proving $\varepsilon(A) \le \exp\left(-c\sqrt{K\log K}\right)$ is now out here: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2607.04157

Your paper appears to be AI generated in other ways than you declare. No human mathematician ever would call a section and the main proposition "The Activation Package". You give no credit to anyone. Some of your sentences/arguments are literally copied from the existing sources and you probably do not even know which.

@Vjeko: It mentions it at the end of the paper: AI assistance was also used in drafting and revising the manuscript, organizing the proof, checking constants and dependencies, suggesting clarifications, and identifying points where additional rigor was needed. The author reviewed, modified, and verified the arguments and assumes full responsibility for the final form and correctness of the paper. *** @Sam: It turns out there are multiple people who, due to AI, write 10 or more single-author papers in just a few months, which might be a bad thing. In your first post, "the Fourier details were performed by GPT-5.5 and might warrant close inspection." Have you inspected it close enough, or you just assume you have to take responsibility, but have indeed barely a clue about it?

I was referring to: "The main conceptual reductions and proof strategy, including the compression framework, the sparse activation method, and the box-moment estimates were developed by the author." This sentence also implies that the author invented the names "compression framework", "sparse activation method", etc., along with developing them, making this whole claim less trustworthy. I also find it practically impossible to actually author that many correct, verified, and digested publications in such a short time, but I don't judge. I am just saying that, if this preprint gets to a journal, it will rightfully trigger several red flags.

Hi Vjeko_Kovac; indeed I've posted many papers on arxiv in the last few weeks, most written with the help of AI (one of the best uses of AI I've found has been generating the LateX for a paper, although I've found the papers have to be cut down substantially); this one has been by far the most AI-involved, as indicated in detail in the acknowledgement sections. As such, this is the paper I have spent the most time personally reviewing (note that my initial comment about the result is from 2.5 weeks ago), and I am indeed confident in the overall veracity of the result. As you suggest, unlike some of my other papers published during this time, I have no plans to send this paper to a journal given my relative lack of involvement in some of the technical details of the proof. Edit: oh and indeed those section/idea names were produced by GPT-5.5, but the credit was described accurately.

I asked Gemini Deep Research and ChatGPT to find references on the problem. In about 10 mins Gemini Deep Research made the following report on the problem. It related the question to the general Subset Sum Problem and to the whole vast literature on the Egyptian fractions, but it did not find anything closely related. It also suggested a few possible approaches: Greedy Algorithm, The Probabilistic Method, and Fourier-Analytic Methods; but all this is easier said than done. :-) I could only run ChatGPT for a short period of time. It mentioned quite a few papers on the Egyptian fractions (like the best underapproximations of 1), but it also admitted that none of them are on multisets with large reciprocal values or on exponentially small gaps around 1, so they are not closely related to the problem.

Does anyone know if Erdős and Graham posed precisely the exponential "gap" for a good reason? For a finite multiset of positive integers $A$ denote\[ R(A) := \sum_{n\in A}\frac{1}{n} \]and\[ \epsilon(A) := \min\Big\{ 1 – R(S) \,:\, S\subseteq A,\, R(S)\leq 1 \Big\}. \]The problem asks if inequality\[ \epsilon(A) \leq e^{- c R(A)} \]holds with some constant $c>0$. Here is a simple example which comes close to showing that this is the best possible. For a positive integer $N$ take\[ A = \bigcup_{p\text{ prime},\ p\leq N} \{\underbrace{p,p,\ldots,p}_{p-1\text{ times}} \}. \]Then\[ R(A) = \sum_{p\text{ prime},\ p\leq N} \frac{p-1}{p} \sim \pi(N) = (1+o(1)) \frac{N}{\log N}, \]while $R(S)\neq1$ for every sub-multiset $S$ of $A$, so\[ \epsilon(A) \geq \prod_{p\text{ prime},\ p\leq N} \frac{1}{p} = \frac{1}{N\#} = e^{-(1+o(1))N}. \]Since $N=(1+o(1))R(A)\log R(A)$, this shows\[ \epsilon(A) \geq e^{- (1+o(1)) R(A) \log R(A)} \]for this particular sequence of examples of multisets $A$. It is not clear to me if a better (e.g., exponential) lower bound is possible, even for these particular multisets $A$. The actual conjectured inequality (the upper bound on $\epsilon(A)$) is a completely different story (many people here would know better), but I first want to clarify to myself how "optimal" it is. :-)
